ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general samosa entry (FOODON_03315671); no specific keema (minced meat) variant exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Samosa — deep-fried pastry with savoury filling
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers samosa as a fried pastry snack; no keema-specific sub-code exists. Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for samosa or keema samosa as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single keema samosa weighs approximately 60–80 g; one piece (70 g) is a standard snack serving.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for samosa and minced-meat preparations. Keema samosa values are adapted from the samosa (meat-filled) and keema (minced meat) entries. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) lamb mince and deep-fried pastry data as ICMR-NIN does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for keema samosa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) samosa with meat filling analogues in the GI database (GI ~48–55 range for mixed fried pastries with protein-rich fillings); (2) the moderating effect of high protein (12.5 g/100 g) and fat (18 g/100 g) from minced meat on glycaemic response of the refined flour shell. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 15.05) / 100 ≈ 7.5 for a 70 g serving (carbohydrate_g = 21.5 × 0.70 = 15.05 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg/100 g) and deep-fried preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit keema samosa consumption due to its high sodium content (420 mg/100 g) and significant fat load from deep-frying; occasional consumption of one small piece is preferable to regular intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content with low estimated GI (~50) moderated by high protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The relatively low estimated GI (~50) and high protein content (12.5 g/100 g) moderate the postprandial glucose response; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one piece and avoid frequent consumption due to the refined flour shell and high caloric density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (18 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~6.2 g/100 g), and deep-fried preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Keema samosa is high in total fat and saturated fat from both the minced red meat filling and deep-frying oil; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food and prioritise lower-fat snack alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential of deep-fried preparation and red meat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried foods and red meat may exacerbate inflammation in some individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; monitor personal response and limit frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keema Samosa is a popular non-vegetarian variant of the classic Indian samosa, widely consumed across North India and among South Asian communities globally. It consists of a crispy, flaky triangular pastry shell made from refined wheat flour (maida), deep-fried until golden-brown, and filled with a spiced minced meat mixture — typically lamb or goat (keema), though chicken is also used. The filling is prepared by sautéing minced meat with onions, ginger, garlic, green chillies, and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, and amchur (dried mango powder). The result is a savoury, aromatic, and slightly spiced filling encased in a shatteringly crisp pastry. Keema samosa is a festive snack particularly associated with Eid celebrations and is also served as a party appetiser or evening tea-time snack. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 290 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 18 g fat, and 21.5 g carbohydrate, with 420 mg sodium. The high protein and fat content from the meat filling moderates the glycaemic response of the refined flour shell (estimated GI ~50, low category). Clinically, it is high in sodium and saturated fat, making it a food to limit for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified meat and contains gluten from the wheat flour pastry.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a keema samosa?
Is keema samosa good for diabetics?
What is the GI of keema samosa?
What is the difference between keema samosa and aloo samosa?
How much protein in keema samosa?
Can I eat keema samosa if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are in keema samosa?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for samosa and minced-meat preparations; no single exact entry for keema samosa exists in the database. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb mince and fried pastry data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans), cholesterol, and moisture data for keema samosa as a composite fried preparation are not disaggregated in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for this specific dish as of 2024; these values are therefore unavailable from any approved source consulted. Saturated_fat_g (6.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked ground lamb (~8 g sat fat/100 g) and deep-fried wheat pastry (~4 g sat fat/100 g), weighted approximately 40:60 by filling-to-pastry ratio. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for keema samosa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving = (50 × 15.05) / 100 ≈ 7.5 for a 70 g serving. Portions in the input JSON contained three identical duplicate entries (100 g each); consolidated to a single representative 70 g serving (one piece) as the default. Halal suitability is conditional on use of halal-certified meat and oil.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Keema Samosa', a precise canonical English name. Hindi local name कीमा समोसा is present. This is an Indian dish; G6 does not mandate Chinese/Malay/Tamil for Indian cuisine. Q1 satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (290), protein_g (12.5), fat_g (18.0), carbohydrate_g (21.5) all present and numeric. nutrient_source cites ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods with USDA SR Legacy supplement for saturated fat. Source linkage is clear.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.2), sodium_mg (420.0), saturated_fat_g (6.2), sugar_g (0.8) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible for a deep-fried meat pastry.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (50), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (7.5), gi_source (University of Sydney GI Database, analogous estimate) all present. Estimation basis documented in gi_source.source_match_notes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI for samosa (FOODON_03315671) used as closest_parent match with gap note. FoodEx2 code A0FJP for fried pastry snack used as closest_parent match with gap note. Both match_type values are now from the allowed set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,450 characters). Covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 10 entries including Hindi script (कीमा समोसा), Urdu script (قیمہ سموسہ), romanized variants (Kheema Samosa, Qeema Samosa), and alternate English names. aliases_by_language populated for english, hindi, and romanized. Self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array — 'Keema Samosa', 'Minced Meat Samosa', 'Meat Samosa', 'Lamb Samosa', 'Mutton Samosa', 'Fried Meat Pastry', 'कीमा समोसा', 'Kheema Samosa', 'Qeema Samosa' all confirmed present. Self-check passed.

G5 satisfied: ontology match_type values corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2, within the allowed enumeration. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN (approved primary source for Indian dishes) with USDA SR Legacy supplement for saturated fat. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with full documentation. All three required health conditions present with clinical guidance. Aliases comprehensive and internally consistent.
